attrs.alt: Permission Creep Index calculation, risk bands, and identity type weighting

content.text: What is the Permission Creep Index?

The Permission Creep Index (PCI) is the primary risk metric produced by Microsoft Entra Permissions Management. It quantifies the gap between what a cloud identity is allowed to do (granted permissions) and what it actually does (used permissions) over a 90-day observation window. A PCI of 0 means the identity uses every permission it has been granted; a PCI of 100 means the identity has never used any of its permissions. The score is normalised to 0-100 and weighted by identity type, then aggregated into a tenant-level score.

The 90-day usage window is fixed. Any permission not exercised in the trailing 90 days is counted as unused, regardless of whether it was used 91 days ago. This means seasonal or quarterly-use identities may show inflated PCI scores — factor this in when prioritising remediation.
PCI Calculation Step by Step

# Conceptual PCI calculation for a single identity
granted_actions = 350   # total distinct cloud API actions the identity CAN call
used_actions    = 12    # actions actually called in the last 90 days

unused_actions = granted_actions - used_actions  # 338
raw_pci        = unused_actions / granted_actions  # 0.966

# EPM normalises raw_pci to 0-100 and applies identity-type weighting
# A human user with raw_pci 0.966 gets a very high weighted PCI (e.g. 97)
# A serverless function with the same ratio gets a lower weighted PCI

# The tenant PCI is the weighted average across ALL identities:
# tenant_pci = sum(identity_pci * weight) / sum(weights)

# Special cases:
# - Identity with ZERO activity in 90 days: PCI = 100 (maximum)
# - Identity with zero unused actions (uses everything granted): PCI = 0
# - Super-identity (>2000 grantable actions): flagged in a separate category
Risk Bands and Remediation Priority
PCI Band
Score Range
Meaning
Recommended Action
Low
Identity uses most of its permissions
Monitor; no immediate action needed
Medium
Significant unused permissions exist
Review and remove clearly unnecessary grants within 30 days
High
Most permissions are unused
Right-size immediately; generate and deploy least-privilege policy
Maximum
Identity has never used any permission in 90 days
Remove all standing permissions; grant on-demand via POD if needed
Identity Type Weighting
Identity Type
Weight in Tenant PCI
Rationale
Human users (members)
Highest
Can be phished or credential-stolen; large blast radius
Service principals
High
Long-lived credentials; often over-provisioned at deployment
Managed identities
Medium
No credentials to steal but permissions often over-scoped
Groups and roles
Lower
Aggregate of member identities; remediate members directly
Serverless / functions
Lowest
Short-lived execution context; permissions typically task-scoped
Super-identities (>2000 actions)
Flagged separately
Treated as a distinct high-severity category regardless of usage
Reading PCI Distribution via Graph API
The PCI distribution endpoint returns the count of identities in each risk band per authorization system. Use this to track tenant-level PCI trends over time.

# Query PCI distribution per authorization system via Graph API
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'PermissionsManagement.Read.All'

# Get all authorization systems
$systems = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

foreach ($sys in $systems) {
    Write-Host "=== $($sys.displayName) ($($sys.authorizationSystemType)) ==="

    # Get PCI distribution (count of identities in each band)
    $dist = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
      -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems/$($sys.id)/microsoft.graph.$($sys.authorizationSystemType)AuthorizationSystem/permissionsCreepIndexDistributions" |
      Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

    $dist | ForEach-Object {
        Write-Host "  $($_.riskLevel): $($_.identityCount) identities (score range $($_.scoreRange))"
    }
}
Listing High-PCI Identities for Remediation
Query identities with PCI >= 67 to build a prioritised right-sizing work queue. Include last active date to distinguish identities that are genuinely inactive from those that are seasonal.
Identifying Super-Identities

Super-identities can perform more than 2,000 distinct cloud actions. Even if their PCI is moderate, the blast radius of a compromise is extreme. Identify and right-size these first regardless of their usage pattern.

# Find super-identities — those that CAN perform more than 2,000 cloud actions
# Super-identities represent the greatest blast radius if compromised

$superIdentities = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems/$azureSysId/microsoft.graph.azureAuthorizationSystem/associatedIdentities/users?`$filter=grantedPermissionsCount ge 2000" |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$superIdentities | Select-Object displayName, userPrincipalName,
  @{N='GrantedActions';E={$_.grantedPermissionsCount}},
  @{N='UsedActions';   E={$_.usedPermissionsCount}},
  @{N='PCI';           E={$_.permissionsCreepIndex.score}}

# A super-identity that also has a high PCI is the highest-priority remediation target:
# high granted count + low usage = massive unnecessary blast radius
Auto-Revoking Completely Inactive Identities

Identities with PCI = 100 have had zero cloud activity for 90 days. After a manager-approval review cycle, these can be automatically remediated: remove all role assignments and rely on POD for any future access needs.

# Workflow: auto-revoke unused permissions for identities with PCI = 100 (never active)
# Run on a schedule (e.g. monthly) after manager approval review

$azureSysId = '<azure-authorization-system-id>'

# Get all completely inactive identities
$inactive = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems/$azureSysId/microsoft.graph.azureAuthorizationSystem/associatedIdentities/users?`$filter=permissionsCreepIndex/score eq 100" |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

foreach ($identity in $inactive) {
    Write-Host "Revoking all Azure RBAC assignments for: $($identity.userPrincipalName)"

    # Get all role assignments for this user at subscription scope
    $assignments = Get-AzRoleAssignment -SignInName $identity.userPrincipalName

    foreach ($assignment in $assignments) {
        Write-Host "  Removing: $($assignment.RoleDefinitionName) at $($assignment.Scope)"
        Remove-AzRoleAssignment -InputObject $assignment -Confirm:$false
    }
}
Write-Host "Remediation complete for $($inactive.Count) inactive identities"
KQL: Tracking Remediation Velocity
Use Entra Audit Logs to count how many right-sizing operations EPM has performed over time. Plot this weekly to demonstrate measurable PCI reduction to stakeholders.

// Track PCI improvement over time using EPM audit log events
// Each time EPM right-sizes an identity, it logs an audit event
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where LoggedByService == 'Permissions Management'
| where OperationName has_any('Revoke', 'Remove', 'Right-size')
| extend Actor  = tostring(InitiatedBy.app.displayName)
| extend Target = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| summarize RemediationCount = count() by bin(TimeGenerated, 7d)
| order by TimeGenerated asc
// Plot this as a bar chart to show remediation velocity over the quarter
Benchmark PCI Targets
There is no universal PCI target, but a phased approach with concrete checkpoints drives consistent improvement. The following illustrative targets reflect a realistic three-month programme.

# Example PCI benchmark targets for a mature cloud security programme
# These are illustrative — set your own based on risk appetite

# Initial state (typical new EPM deployment):
#   Human users:         avg PCI ~85  (most have Global Reader or Contributor + years of unused actions)
#   Service principals:  avg PCI ~70  (often granted Owner at subscription scope)
#   Managed identities:  avg PCI ~60  (more likely to be task-specific)

# 30-day target (after removing inactive identities and obvious over-grants):
#   Human users:         avg PCI ~65
#   Service principals:  avg PCI ~50

# 90-day target (after right-sizing all High PCI identities):
#   Human users:         avg PCI ~40
#   Service principals:  avg PCI ~30

# Steady-state target (ongoing governance):
#   Tenant PCI:          <= 33 (Low band)
#   Zero super-identities with PCI > 50
#   Zero identities inactive > 90 days with any standing permissions
Common PCI Interpretation Pitfalls
PCI can be misleading for certain identity patterns. Understanding these cases prevents mis-prioritisation of remediation effort and avoids breaking legitimate workflows.

# Common PCI interpretation pitfalls and how to handle them

# Pitfall 1: Break-glass accounts
# Problem: Emergency access accounts (break-glass) intentionally sit idle
#          They will always show PCI = 100
# Fix:     Tag break-glass accounts as exceptions in EPM
#          Exclude them from automated remediation workflows
#          Audit their permission scope separately (should be tightly scoped)

# Pitfall 2: Batch/seasonal workloads
# Problem: A Lambda/Function that runs quarterly payroll shows PCI = 100
#          for the 80 days between runs
# Fix:     Review usage over a 365-day window rather than 90 days
#          Grant permissions via POD at run time and revoke after

# Pitfall 3: Shared service accounts
# Problem: A service account shared by multiple teams may show low PCI
#          because its combined usage covers many permissions
#          but each individual team only needs a subset
# Fix:     Replace shared accounts with per-team managed identities
#          Each gets its own least-privilege policy

# Pitfall 4: Inherited permissions via groups
# Problem: A user in a large security group inherits 500 actions
#          EPM counts all inherited actions as granted
#          but the user may only need 10 of them
# Fix:     Assign direct RBAC roles scoped to needed resources
#          Remove over-broad group memberships
KQL: Detecting New High-PCI Identities Week-Over-Week

Build a weekly governance query that surfaces identities whose PCI crossed into the High band since the previous report. This focuses remediation on newly degraded identities rather than reviewing the entire High list every week.

// Weekly PCI governance report — identify new high-PCI identities since last week
// Requires EPM audit log events when new identities are discovered or PCI changes
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where LoggedByService == 'Permissions Management'
| where OperationName has 'permissionsCreepIndex'
| extend IdentityUPN = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName)
| extend NewPCI      = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties
    | where Name == 'permissionsCreepIndex' | project NewValue | limit 1)
| extend OldPCI      = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties
    | where Name == 'permissionsCreepIndex' | project OldValue | limit 1)
| where tolong(NewPCI) >= 67 and tolong(OldPCI) < 67
| project TimeGenerated, IdentityUPN, OldPCI, NewPCI
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Multi-Cloud PCI Summary Report
Generate a single cross-cloud PCI summary to present to stakeholders — shows identity count in each risk band per cloud provider and the percentage of identities in the High band.

# Compare PCI across all three clouds in a single summary report
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'PermissionsManagement.Read.All'

$systems = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

$report = foreach ($sys in $systems) {
    $typeKey = $sys.authorizationSystemType + 'AuthorizationSystem'
    $dist = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
      -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/external/authorizationSystems/$($sys.id)/microsoft.graph.$typeKey/permissionsCreepIndexDistributions" |
      Select-Object -ExpandProperty value

    $low    = ($dist | Where-Object { $_.riskLevel -eq 'low'    }).identityCount
    $medium = ($dist | Where-Object { $_.riskLevel -eq 'medium' }).identityCount
    $high   = ($dist | Where-Object { $_.riskLevel -eq 'high'   }).identityCount
    $total  = $low + $medium + $high

    [PSCustomObject]@{
        Cloud       = $sys.displayName
        Type        = $sys.authorizationSystemType
        Low         = $low
        Medium      = $medium
        High        = $high
        Total       = $total
        HighPct     = if ($total -gt 0) { [math]::Round(100*$high/$total,1) } else { 0 }
    }
}
$report | Format-Table -AutoSize
PCI in the Context of Zero Trust
Zero Trust Principle
PCI Contribution
Verify explicitly
PCI identifies who has permissions — a prerequisite for knowing what to verify
Use least-privilege access
Reducing PCI directly implements least-privilege at scale across multi-cloud
Assume breach
Low PCI limits blast radius: a compromised identity can only affect resources it actually uses
Continuous monitoring
PCI recalculates on each EPM discovery cycle — drift from least-privilege is detected automatically
Summary

The Permission Creep Index (0-100) measures unused permissions as a fraction of granted permissions over a 90-day window. PCI 100 means a completely inactive identity; PCI 0 means perfect least-privilege.
Risk bands: Low (0-33) — monitor; Medium (34-66) — review within 30 days; High (67-99) — right-size immediately; 100 — remove all standing permissions.
Human users carry the highest weight in the tenant PCI calculation; super-identities (>2,000 grantable actions) are flagged as a separate high-severity category.
The 90-day window means seasonal identities may show artificially high PCI — factor usage patterns in before automated remediation.
Build a remediation queue by filtering on PCI >= 67, prioritising super-identities and completely inactive accounts (PCI 100) for immediate action.
Track remediation velocity with KQL over Entra Audit Logs to demonstrate measurable PCI reduction to stakeholders over a quarter.
